paragraphs.body: Facing the Past: Steps to Overcome the Fear Left by Bullying  Introduction Fear from past experiences, like school bullying, can have a lasting impact on our mental and physical health. Even years after bullying ends, certain triggers can bring back painful memories and cause reactions like trembling, difficulty breathing, or overwhelming anxiety. These responses may seem irrational, but they are deeply rooted in trauma.

paragraphs.body: This essay focuses on overcoming fear caused by school bullying. It highlights two key solutions: seeking professional help and practicing self-compassion. These approaches are essential for healing, allowing victims to regain control of their emotions and move forward confidently.

Research Background School bullying affects millions of students around the world and leaves emotional scars that can last into adulthood. Research shows that victims of bullying often experience anxiety, depression, and PTSD-like symptoms. According to a study published in Child and Adolescent Psychiatry and Mental Health, bullying victims may feel unsafe even in normal situations, reacting with trembling, sweating, or panic. These physical responses come from the body’s survival instincts.

annotations.text: Research shows that victims of bullying often experience anxiety, depression, and PTSD-like symptoms. According to a study published in Child and Adolescent Psychiatry and Mental Health,
annotations.comment: Please use in-text citations to justify your claims.

paragraphs.body: For college students, these symptoms can disrupt academics, friendships, and personal growth. Hymel and Swearer (2015) point out that bullying is not just a childhood problem—it has long-term psychological effects. From my own experience, seeing someone who bullied me in high school triggers strong emotional and physical reactions. It reminds me of the feelings of helplessness I had during that time.

However, mental health challenges like this are often overlooked, especially in traditional Chinese families. Many parents dismiss their children’s struggles, saying things like, “Don’t think about it,” or “Just move on.” This cultural attitude creates a barrier to seeking help, making recovery even more difficult.

Seeking Professional Help Professional help, such as therapy or medication, is one of the most effective ways to address the fear caused by bullying. Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) is especially helpful because it teaches people how to challenge negative thoughts and develop coping skills. For example, someone who feels anxious about seeing a bully again can learn strategies to stay calm and feel more in control.

annotations.comment: The headings should be centered and in bold.

paragraphs.body: Medication can also help manage severe symptoms like panic attacks or physical trembling. These treatments are not a sign of weakness but tools to help the body and mind heal. Personally, visiting a school counselor was a turning point for me. I learned techniques to manage my anxiety, like breathing exercises and mindfulness practices. These small steps made a big difference in my daily life.

Unfortunately, seeking help is not always easy. Corrigan and Watson (2002) explain that mental health stigma often prevents people from getting the support they need. In traditional Chinese culture, admitting to a mental health issue is sometimes seen as a failure. When I told my parents about my anxiety, they said, “You’re fine. Stop overthinking it.” While their response came from a place of care, it felt dismissive and made me hesitate to seek further help. To truly recover, we need to challenge these cultural stigmas and create a more supportive environment for mental health.

paragraphs.body: Practicing Self-Compassion Self-compassion is another important step in overcoming fear from past bullying. Many victims blame themselves for their emotional and physical reactions, thinking they should be “stronger” or “over it” by now. This kind of self-criticism only makes things worse, deepening feelings of shame and anxiety.

annotations.comment: Same as above. Shorter paragraphs can be consolidated to build a more sustained argument.

paragraphs.body: Juvonen and Graham (2014) emphasize that self-compassion helps individuals accept their emotions without judgment. It allows them to treat themselves with kindness and recognize that their reactions are normal. For a long time, I struggled with self-blame. When my hands trembled or I felt panicked, I would think, “Why can’t I just let it go?” These thoughts made me feel weak and frustrated.

Over time, I learned to view my reactions differently. Trauma responses, like trembling or shortness of breath, are not signs of weakness. They are the body’s natural way of protecting itself. Instead of criticizing myself, I started practicing self-compassion by acknowledging my feelings and reminding myself that it’s okay to be scared. Positive affirmations like “This is just my body trying to keep me safe” helped me feel calmer and more in control. Practical strategies like journaling and mindfulness also played a big role in my recovery. Writing down my thoughts allowed me to process my emotions in a safe and private way. Mindfulness exercises, such as focusing on my breathing, helped me calm my body during moments of distress. For example, if I felt anxious after seeing someone who reminded me of my bully, I would focus on taking slow, deep breaths and grounding myself in the present moment. These small habits have helped me regain confidence and reduce my anxiety over time.

Conclusion Overcoming fear from past bullying is not an easy journey, but it is possible. Professional help provides tools to manage trauma, while self-compassion allows victims to embrace their emotions without guilt or shame. Together, these approaches can help individuals heal and reclaim their sense of safety and strength.

As college students, we have the power to rewrite our stories. By seeking help, breaking the stigma around mental health, and treating ourselves with kindness, we can move beyond our fears and build a brighter future. Though the process of healing is challenging, it is also an opportunity for growth, resilience, and self-discovery.

References • Hymel, S., & Swearer, S. M. (2015). Four decades of research on school bullying: An introduction. American Psychologist, 70(4), 293– 299. https://www.apa.org/pubs/journals/releases/amp-a0038928.pdf • Charles, Z. L. (2023). Bullying in schools: The power of bullies and the plight of victims. Annual Review of Psychology, 65, 159–185. https://www.annualreviews.org/doi/10.1146/annurev-psych-010213-115030 • Arslan, G., Allen, K. A., & Tanhan, A. (2021).
